summary: The papers focus on advancing detection, tracking, and neutralization capabilities for small unmanned aerial systems, with particular emphasis on real-time performance under challenging conditions. One study introduces a lightweight neural network architecture optimized for detecting small, fast-moving drones in cluttered environments, addressing issues of size and visual contrast. Another proposes a heterogeneous observer-follower framework that enables high-resolution UAV tracking on embedded platforms by resolving the trade-off between image resolution and processing speed. A third paper develops a multi-physics simulation framework for designing high-power microwave counter-UAS systems, aiming to neutralize autonomous drones without kinetic or RF jamming methods. Additionally, research on visual cooperative tracking integrates open-path gas sensing with drone navigation for environmental monitoring, while a cryptographic framework enhances secure telemetry transmission for resource-constrained IoT devices, including small drones.
